title: Indonesian Gado Gado

subs.name: paneer cubes, shallow-fried until golden
subs.notes: widely available in India and takes the same treatment

subs.name: any seed butter (sunflower, pumpkin) for a peanut-free version
1.5 tbsp lemon juice plus 0.5 tsp amchur
subs.notes: gives tartness without the tamarind funk

steps.text: Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Add potato and cook 8–10 minutes until just tender but not falling apart. In the last 3 minutes, add carrot and beans; in the final minute, add cabbage. Drain all vegetables and arrange on a large plate.
Heat 2 tbsp oil in a frying pan on medium-high. Fry the tofu cubes for 4–5 minutes, turning, until golden on all sides. Drain on paper and add to the plate alongside the cucumber and halved eggs.

For the peanut sauce, blend roasted peanuts and garlic to a coarse paste. Add tamarind pulp, jaggery, chilli powder, soy sauce, lemon juice and 80 ml warm water. Blend until smooth and pourable, adjusting water for consistency.

Transfer the peanut sauce to a small saucepan. Warm on low heat, stirring, for 2–3 minutes until it glosses and the raw garlic smell is gone. Taste and adjust sweetness (jaggery), sourness (tamarind) and salt.

Pour the warm peanut sauce generously over the arranged vegetables, tofu and eggs. Serve immediately while the sauce is warm and the vegetables are still slightly warm. In Indonesia, gado gado is dressed at the table so each guest can control the ratio.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tofu → paneer cubes, shallow-fried until golden (widely available in India and takes the same treatment); peanut → any seed butter (sunflower, pumpkin) for a peanut-free version; tamarind → 1.5 tbsp lemon juice plus 0.5 tsp amchur (gives tartness without the tamarind funk); jaggery → 2 tsp sugar or honey
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Everything in this recipe is available at Indian grocery stores. Use roasted peanuts (verkadalai) from any snack section. Tamarind pulp (puli) is standard in South Indian kitchens. Tofu (paneer mould bean curd) is available in larger cities; paneer is an excellent substitute. Jaggery (vellam/gur) replaces palm sugar exactly. A kadai works as well as any pot for blanching.
